metadata.title: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The exponential ruler is beautiful: the wavefunction decays as e to the minus kappa d in the gap, current goes as e to the minus 2 kappa d, so one extra angstrom drops it by an order of magnitude and only the tip atom matters. I want to work the sensitivity. Please: (1) use kappa about 1.025 per angstrom to confirm the factor near e squared per angstrom, (2) compute how a 0.5-angstrom step changes the current, and (3) show why raising the barrier work function sharpens the atomic resolution.

props.command: The exponential ruler is beautiful: the wavefunction decays as e to the minus kappa d in the gap, current goes as e to the minus 2 kappa d, so one extra angstrom drops it by an order of magnitude and only the tip atom matters. I want to work the sensitivity. Please: (1) use kappa about 1.025 per angstrom to confirm the factor near e squared per angstrom, (2) compute how a 0.5-angstrom step changes the current, and (3) show why raising the barrier work function sharpens the atomic resolution.
props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a

beats.narration_text: Move a metal tip one angstrom closer to a surface and the current doubles. One angstrom — a tenth of a nanometer. The tunneling current is so sensitive to distance that the microscope can feel the height of a single atom.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: how does a scanning tunneling microscope work?

beats.narration_text: A scanning tunneling microscope can image individual atoms. Its secret is a number: moving the tip one atom's-width closer changes the current by a factor of ten.

card.copy: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: STM tip hovering a few angstroms above a metal surface — show the vacuum gap, electrons tunneling as arrows (TEAL), label tip-surface distance in angstroms"

beats.narration_text: The tip never touches. It hovers a few ångströms above the surface — closer than the width of a single atom — and electrons tunnel across the gap.
The current is flowing across a vacuum gap. Classically, electrons can't cross a gap wider than their energy allows. So why does any current flow at all?

card.copy:
The tip never touches.
Classically, no current should flow.
Why does it?

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: wave function decaying exponentially inside a vacuum barrier — show psi = e^(-kappa*d) with kappa ~1 per angstrom, plot the amplitude falling as distance increases"

beats.narration_text: Inside the barrier, the wavefunction doesn't stop. It decays exponentially: psi falls as e to the minus kappa-d, where d is the gap width and kappa is about one per ångström for typical metal work functions.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: plot tunneling current I ~ e^(-2*kappa*d) vs gap distance d — show the exponential drop, mark kappa = 1.025 per angstrom for 4 eV barrier, label the curve"

beats.narration_text: The tunneling current is proportional to the squared amplitude: I proportional to e to the minus 2-kappa-d. Kappa is roughly 1.025 per ångström for a four electron-volt barrier — typical of most metals.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: adding 1 angstrom to the gap — show the exponent growing by 2*kappa*1 ≈ 2, current dropping by factor e^2 ≈ 7 to 10, bar chart comparison of before and after"

beats.narration_text: Add one extra ångström to the gap. The exponent grows by 2 kappa times one, which is about 2. So the current drops by e squared — a factor of roughly seven to ten. One ångström changes the current by an order of magnitude.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: STM tip showing the single closest atom dominating — the front atom is 1 angstrom closer than the next atoms, its current contribution 7-10x larger, other tip atoms faded"

beats.narration_text: That extreme sensitivity is exactly what gives atomic resolution. The atom on the very tip of the probe is one ångström closer to the surface than the atoms one step back. Its current contribution is seven to ten times larger. The rest of the tip is nearly invisible.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: STM tip scanning sideways over surface atoms — show current spiking as each atom passes under the tip, trace the current vs position to map the atomic topography"

beats.narration_text: As the tip scans sideways, each surface atom it passes over is the closest object for a moment. The current spikes, then falls. The current trace maps the atomic topography directly — peaks of current above atoms, valleys between them.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: the same exponential tunneling decay appearing in three contexts — STM (electrons), alpha decay (nucleus), photosynthesis (electron transfer) — connect them with the same e^(-kappa*d) formula"

beats.narration_text: This is the same exponential that governs alpha decay in nuclei and electron transfer in photosynthesis. Quantum tunneling is not a curiosity. It is how biology extracts energy from sunlight, and how we image the atomic world.

The wavefunction decays in the gap as e to the minus kappa-d. Current falls as e to the minus 2-kappa-d. One ångström — one factor of ten. That exponential ruler gives the STM atomic resolution without ever touching.

card.copy:
psi ~ e^(-kd). Current ~ e^(-2kd).
1 angstrom = factor of ~7-10.
Exponential ruler: atomic resolution.

beats.narration_text: The exponential ruler is why STM works at all. Paste this into Claude and see how steep the curve is.

props.segment: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
props.command: claude "simulate STM tunneling current as a function of tip-surface distance — show the exponential dependence and explain why one angstrom change means a factor-of-two current change"

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The wavefunction decays in the gap as e to the minus kappa-d. Current falls as e to the minus 2-kappa-d. One ångström — one factor of ten. That exponential ruler gives the STM atomic resolution without ever touching. The exponential ruler is why STM works at all. Paste this into Claude and see how steep the curve is.
